digest.top_line: FIRESTORM: dose-escalated RT (BED ≥79.2 Gy) 5yr PFS 65.8% vs 38.8%, HR 0.40, in high-risk meningioma; no OS gain.
digest.tldr: Meningioma RT carried the day: FIRESTORM's dose escalation (HR 0.40) and RTOG 0539's mature 12yr risk-adapted PFS both argue for titrating RT intensity to risk, though neither moves OS. In breast, RAPCHEM backs response-adapted RNI omission after NAC (10yr LRR 2.9%).

sites.intro: Two meningioma cohorts converge on risk- and dose-stratified RT: RTOG 0539 (prospective, 12yr f/u) and FIRESTORM (dose escalation in high-risk, non-randomized IPD).

studies.name: RTOG 0539
studies.tldr: 10yr PFS 85.2%/72.2%/42.5% for low (observed)/intermediate (54Gy)/high (60Gy)-risk meningioma; mature 12yr f/u supports risk-adapted RT.

details.text: 📊 10yr cumulative incidence of progression (competing-risk, death censored)

details: 🔍 Prospective phase 2 (NRG/RTOG cooperative group), risk-adapted; 244 consented, 165 protocol-treated
🔍 Risk group set by WHO grade, resection extent (GTR/STR), recurrence status; central path review

details.text: 📐 30 fractions in both RT cohorts (54 Gy intermediate, 60 Gy high)

details: 🔍 Median f/u 12.1 / 12.0 / 11.1 yrs (low/intermediate/high); median PFS not reached low + intermediate
⚠️ No randomized observation control in the RT cohorts, so RT's absolute benefit over surgery alone isn't isolable

verdict.rationale: Prospective NRG/RTOG phase 2, mature 12yr f/u, reinforces risk-adapted standard (observe low-risk, RT grade 2/3); no randomized observation control so RT's absolute benefit isn't isolated.
verdict.audience: WHO grade 1-3 meningioma, risk-adapted by grade/resection/recurrence

studies.significance: The actionable read is intermediate-risk: GTR grade 2 or recurrent grade 1 to 54Gy/30fx reached 72.2% 10yr PFS, but with no observation control RT's absolute benefit stays unquantified. High-risk 42.5% PFS at 60Gy marks the current ceiling, a benchmark for dose-escalation trials.
studies.monday_clinic: In newly-diagnosed WHO grade 2 meningioma after GTR, this benchmarks adjuvant 54Gy at 72.2% 10yr PFS but does not settle RT vs observation; low-risk grade 1 post-resection is supported for observation, not RT.
open_questions: Adjuvant RT vs observation for gross-totally-resected grade 2 meningioma
Dose escalation for high-risk grade 3 meningioma
Molecular risk stratification beyond WHO grade

related_trials.brief_title: Efficacy of Postoperative Radiotherapy for Atypical Meningioma Without Venous Sinus Invasion After Gross-total Resection
related_trials.brief_summary:
Postoperative adjuvant radiotherapy is a key component of comprehensive treatment of meningioma. However, for atypical meningioma after total resection, there is still a huge controversy in patients who need adjuvant radiotherapy after surgery.

Many scholars have focused on this problem and carried out some small-scale retrospective studies, but they have contradictory results. Some of the studies found that postoperative adjuvant radiotherapy could not improve the prognosis of patients, but…
related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Patients who underwent gross total resection at each center and were diagnosed as atypical meningioma (WHO class II) according to the WHO 2016 diagnostic criteria.
* Gross total resection was defined as modified Simpson 1-3 and confirmed by postoperative magnetic resonance…

related_trials.answers_question: Dose escalation for high-risk grade 3 meningioma

studies.name: FIRESTORM
studies.tldr: DE-RT (BED ≥79.2 Gy) improved 5yr PFS 65.8% vs 38.8%, HR 0.40 (0.24-0.69), in high-risk meningioma; no OS benefit.
details: 📊 1° endpoint PFS favored DE-RT (BED ≥79.2 Gy, ≈66 Gy/33fx) over SD-RT, log-rank P=.0022
🔍 Individual patient-level meta-analysis, 7 institutions, N=248 (59 DE-RT, 189 SD-RT); non-randomized, propensity/IPTW-adjusted
🔍 Population: 75.8% WHO grade 2, 41.5% recurrent, 75.2% subtotal resection
🔗 Largest gain in STR/Simpson 4-5: HR 0.55 (0.36-0.84), P=.006; 5yr PFS 63.3% vs 38.4%

details.text: 🔍 Held on sensitivity (SD-RT limited to 59.4-60 Gy, prior-RT excluded): HR 0.37 (0.19-0.73), P=.004
⚠️ No OS benefit: DE-RT not significantly associated with OS (MVA HR 0.66, 0.33-1.34; IPTW HR 0.77, 0.41-1.45), both NS

verdict.rationale: Retrospective IPD meta-analysis, non-randomized: 59 DE-RT pts, propensity/IPTW-adjusted. PFS benefit but no OS gain; residual confounding and selection bias plausible.
verdict.audience: High-risk meningioma (WHO grade 2/3), post-op, mostly subtotal resection

studies.significance: Dose is the transferable read: DE-RT meant BED ≥79.2 Gy (≈66 Gy/33fx) vs standard 59.4-60 Gy, and the largest PFS gain sat in the STR/Simpson 4-5 subgroup (HR 0.55, 0.36-0.84), the ~75% a radonc actually treats. Any-grade radionecrosis rose (33.9% vs 13.2%) while G3+ stayed low (5.1% vs 3.2%). Moves the postop dose-escalation question, not the OS case.
studies.monday_clinic: In high-risk meningioma (WHO grade 2, subtotally resected) facing postop RT, this retrospective signal supports weighing dose escalation toward BED ≥79.2 Gy for PFS; it doesn't extend to gross-totally-resected pts or establish an OS benefit.
open_questions: OS benefit of dose escalation in high-risk meningioma
Randomized confirmation of postoperative dose escalation
Carbon-ion vs photon for dose-escalated meningioma RT

related_trials.brief_title: Somatostatin Receptor PET Imaging to Guide Radiotherapy Dose Escalation in High Risk Meningiomas.
related_trials.brief_summary: High-risk meningiomas always require postsurgical radiation treatment. Recent evidence has shown that increased radiation therapy dose may be associated with increased intracranial control of disease. In order to better define the volume of radiation treatment, the addition of PET imaging with somatostatin receptor tracers adds additional information compared to encephalon MRI with MoC alone.The present study aims to investigate whether radiation treatment with higher doses than the standard…

interventions.name: Dose escalation of radiation therapy, based on somatostatin receptor PET imaging

sites.intro: RAPCHEM tests RT de-escalation by post-NAC nodal response, including RNI omission; descriptive cohort, no randomized comparator yet.

studies.name: RAPCHEM (BOOG 2010-03)
studies.tldr: 10yr LRR 2.9% (24/838) with RT de-escalated by nodal response to NAC; ≤3.2% across all risk groups, including RT omission in ypN0.

details.text: 📊 10yr locoregional recurrence by post-NAC nodal risk group (overall 2.9%, 24/838)

rows: Breast RT if BCS, none if mastectomy

details: 🔍 Prospective cohort, N=848, 17 Dutch centers (2011-2015); cT<5cm cN1 breast, neoadjuvant chemo then surgery
🔍 Risk set by residual nodal disease after NAC: node-negative (low), 1-3 positive (intermediate), ≥4 positive (high)
⚠️ No RT-vs-no-RT comparator; single descriptive cohort, can't establish de-escalation non-inferiority
⚠️ Low LRR partly reflects favorable biology of good chemo responders, not proof RT was safely cut
⚠️ Most pts had ALND; less used now (SLNB era), limits transfer to current axillary staging
🔗 Awaits NSABP randomized trial (NCT01872975), results ~3yr, the confirmatory de-escalation RCT

verdict.rationale: Large prospective multicenter cohort (no randomized RT-omission comparator) backing emerging risk-adapted de-escalation; reassuring 10yr LRR but not definitive, awaits NSABP RCT (NCT01872975).

studies.significance: The RT read: nodal-response-guided de-escalation held up at 10yr. Intermediate-risk pts (ypN 1-3) had regional nodal irradiation omitted yet LRR was 3.2%; low-risk ypN0 got breast-only RT or none post-mastectomy at 2.4%. Moves the RNI-omission decision in good NAC responders (most had ALND).
studies.monday_clinic: In cN1 breast pts who become ypN0 or ypN1-3 after neoadjuvant chemo and ALND, this supports omitting regional nodal RT; it does not extend to sentinel-node-staged axillae now common in practice.
open_questions: Non-inferiority of RT de-escalation vs standard RT (NSABP RCT pending)
Does result hold with sentinel-node staging rather than ALND?
Tumor biology factors refining recurrence risk beyond nodal response

related_trials.brief_title: The T-REX Trial: Tailored Regional External Beam Radiotherapy in Clinically Node-negative Breast Cancer Patients With 1-2 Sentinel Node Macrometastases.
related_trials.brief_summary:
T-REX is a randomized multicenter, non-inferiority trial.

Aim: To evaluate whether regional radiotherapy may safely be omitted in clinically node negative breast cancer patients with one or two sentinel node macrometastases and an estrogen receptor positive, HER2-negative tumor. Leading to an improved quality of life and reduced side effects, without worsening recurrence free survival at five years.

Intervention: Patients will be randomized to locoregional radiotherapy towards the…

papers.title: Long-Term Analysis of NRG Oncology RTOG 0539: A Phase II Trial of Observation for Low-Risk Meningioma and Radiotherapy for Intermediate- and High-Risk Meningioma
papers.abstract: NRG Oncology RTOG 0539 was a prospective phase II trial of risk-adapted radiotherapy for patients with WHO grade 1-3 meningioma. Low-risk (group 1, n = 60) was defined as a grade 1 tumor after gross total resection or subtotal resection (GTR/STR) and prospectively monitored. Intermediate-risk (group 2, n = 52) was defined as recurrent grade 1 or newly diagnosed grade 2 tumor after GTR and treated with radiotherapy (54 Gy). High-risk (group 3, n = 53) included a newly diagnosed grade 2 tumor after STR, newly diagnosed grade 3 tumor, or recurrent grade 2 or 3 tumor and treated with radiotherapy (60 Gy). Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were estimated using the Kaplan-Meier method. The median follow-up times for the low-, intermediate-, and high-risk cohorts were 12.1, 12.0, and 11.1 years, respectively. The 10-year PFS and OS rates for the low-, intermediate-, and high-risk cohorts were 85.2% and 94.1%, 72.2% and 84.7%, and 42.5% and 51.1%, respectively. Five patients (9.6%) and eight patients (15.1%) had a grade 3+ toxicity attributed to radiotherapy in the intermediate- and high-risk cohorts, respectively. The long-term outcomes using this risk-adapted approach support observation for low-risk patients, inform radiotherapy patient selection and practice standards for intermediate- and high-risk patients, and provide comparative benchmarks for future trials.

papers.title: Improved Progression-Free Survival Following Dose-Escalated Versus Standard-Dose Postoperative Radiation Therapy for High-Risk Meningiomas: An International Multicenter Individual Patient–Level Meta-Analysis (FIRESTORM)
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: We performed an individual patient-level meta-analysis of high-risk meningiomas to compare the outcomes of dose-escalated radiation therapy (DE-RT) versus standard-dose postoperative radiation therapy (SD-RT).

METHODS AND MATERIALS: A total of 7 institutions participated. DE-RT was defined as treatment with a biologically effective dose of ≥79.2 Gy (equivalent of 66 Gy in 33 fractions). We compared progression-free survival (PFS) with DE-RT versus SD-RT via Kaplan-Meier analysis and log-rank t tests, a Cox proportional hazards multivariable model, and propensity score analyses with inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW). We also compared incidences of central nervous system radionecrosis (RN) with DE-RT versus SD-RT.

RESULTS: The analysis included 248 patients with high-risk meningioma (59 received DE-RT and 189 received SD-RT). One hundred and eighty-eight cases (75.8%) were World Health Organization grade 2, and 103 cases (41.5%) were recurrent meningiomas. Extent of resection was subtotal resection in 182 of 248 (75.2%). Three- and 5-year PFS rates were 62.8% (95% CI, 55.8%-69.0%) and 45.0% (95% CI, 37.3%-52.3%), respectively. DE-RT was associated with superior PFS rates (P = .0022), with 3-year (86.4% vs 55.6%) and 5-year (65.8% vs 38.8%) PFS rates favoring DE-RT. On multivariable analysis, DE-RT was associated with superior PFS (hazard ratio, 0.40; 95% CI, 0.24-0.69; P = .001). On IPTW, DE-RT continued to be associated with superior PFS (hazard ratio, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.24-0.83; P = .01). A greater incidence of any grade RN was observed following DE-RT (20 of 59; 33.9%) versus SD-RT (25 of 189; 13.2%) (P = .001) but with similar grade 3 or greater RN events (DE-RT 5.1% vs SD-RT 3.2%).

CONCLUSIONS: DE-RT resulted in superior PFS for patients with high-risk meningiomas over SD-RT without an increase in severe toxicities.
